---
title: "world models"
subtitle: "paper review"
date: "2026-04-02"
image: "world-model.png"
---
This is the beginning of my journey, starting with a dive into machine learning broadly and [world models](https://worldmodels.github.io/) more specifically. What an interesting place to start.

A few things stood out to me in this paper. First, it wasn't a single monolithic model — it was a *system* of models, each playing a distinct role in the overall architecture. As someone new to this, I found it fascinating that the design was inspired by the human cognitive system and our mental models. My take: we as humans can't model the entire world, so our brains have learned, over time, to maintain an efficient abstract representation of both space and time.

> "The image of the world around us, which we carry in our head, is just a model. Nobody in his head imagines all the world, government or country. He has only selected concepts, and relationships between them, and uses those to represent the real system." — Jay Wright Forrester (pioneer in system dynamics)

Each model in the system was architecturally different and trained for a specific purpose.

**V — The Vision Model.** The visual model is a Convolutional Variational Autoencoder (ConvVAE) that compresses a 64×64×3 image into a 32-dimensional latent vector. That's 12,288 values compressed down to 32 — a 384× reduction. This kind of compression naturally causes loss, but the goal isn't pixel-perfect reconstruction; it's to retain only the relevant features of the image in latent space. The paper does mention instances where important information was lost during encoding and decoding. One detail I found interesting: the final layer maps to a Gaussian distribution parameterized by a mean and standard deviation, rather than a single deterministic point. In sum, the vision model captures a single moment *t* as a compact probabilistic snapshot in latent space.

**M — The Memory Model.** The second model is a Mixture Density Network combined with a Recurrent Neural Network (MDN-RNN). The backbone is an LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory), a specialized RNN designed to *remember* sequences of events. The hidden state size was 256 for the car racing environment and 512 for Doom. At each time step, M takes two inputs: what the agent is currently seeing — the latent vector *z* at time *t* (32 dimensions) — and the action it took, *a* (3 dimensions), for a combined 35-dimensional input. By processing these together over time, M learns cause and effect and uses that information to predict the future. Its output is a hidden state vector (256 dimensions) that serves as a compressed memory of everything relevant that's happened so far. Since this memory is fixed-size, information is necessarily and purposefully forgotten along the way.

**C — The Controller.** The final model is the controller, and it's by far the smallest. It takes the current latent image (*z*, 32 dimensions) and the current memory state (*h*, 256 dimensions) for a total of 288 inputs. It multiplies this by a learned weight matrix and applies a tanh function to produce 3 bounded action values: steering (−1 for full left to +1 for full right), acceleration (0 to 1), and braking (0 to 1).

One of the most novel ideas in the paper is the clean separation between the world model (VAE + LSTM) and the controller, along with the concept of *dream training*. Instead of training exclusively in the real environment, the agent can train inside its own learned world model — essentially dreaming. The dream environment is made more robust by introducing a done signal (so the agent can experience death) and a temperature parameter that controls uncertainty in the LSTM's predictions, making the dreams harder or easier. This lets the controller learn from a huge volume of imagined experience without ever touching the real environment.

Overall, a fascinating paper. I'm excited to replicate it on the LunarLander game.
